---
title: "Get started"
subtitle: "From event timings to a usable fMRI design matrix."
jupyter: python3
---

## Install

```bash
pip install fmrimod
```

For local development from this checkout:

```bash
pip install -e .
```

## The 30-Second Tour

Start with an event table and a sampling frame. The event table stores
experimental timing; the sampling frame defines the acquired time grid.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import fmrimod as fm
from fmrimod.design_colmap import design_colmap

events = pd.DataFrame({
    "onset": [0, 12, 24, 36],
    "condition": ["face", "scene", "face", "scene"],
})

sf = fm.SamplingFrame(blocklens=40, TR=1.0)
```

Use a formula to turn those events into task regressors. This example creates
one canonical SPM HRF regressor per condition.

```{python}
model = fm.event_model(
    'onset ~ hrf(condition, basis="spmg1")',
    data=events,
    sampling_frame=sf,
)

model.column_names
```

The resulting matrix has one row per acquired sample and one column per model
regressor.

```{python}
design = model.design_matrix
design.shape
```

## What Just Happened

`SamplingFrame` defines the time axis, `event_model()` parses the formula and
constructs event terms, and `design_matrix` holds the convolved regressors ready
for downstream GLM fitting.

You can inspect the design surface with the same top-level API:

```{python}
colmap = design_colmap(model)
colmap[["col", "name", "role", "model_source"]]
```

## Next Steps

- [Tutorial: design matrices](tutorials/design-matrices.qmd)
- [API reference](reference/index.qmd)
